title: Fix NaN loss on empty input array in neural-net.ts:213
description:
neural-net.ts:213 computes `totalLoss / inputs.length` which produces NaN when inputs is empty (division by 0). The accuracy computation on line 214 IS guarded (`inputs.length > 0 ? ... : 0`) but loss is not.

Fix: Add same guard for loss: `loss: inputs.length > 0 ? totalLoss / inputs.length : 0`
acceptanceCriteria: train() with empty inputs array returns loss=0, accuracy=0
Existing training behavior unchanged for non-empty inputs
Add unit test: NeuralNetwork.train([], [], 0.1, 5, callback) produces loss=0

title: Fix epsilon-greedy initial arm bias — always picks Arm 0 first
description:
multi-armed-bandit.ts:98-107: When all arms are unpulled (pulls[i]=0), the exploit branch sets `est = Infinity` for all arms. The loop picks the FIRST arm with Infinity, which is always Arm 0. This creates a systematic bias toward the first arm. UCB1 avoids this by cycling through unpulled arms (line 143-145).

Fix: When exploiting and all arms tied, randomize selection among tied arms. Or: force one pull per arm before any exploitation (like UCB1 does).
acceptanceCriteria: First N rounds (where N = arm count) pull each arm once in random order
Or: when multiple arms tied at best value, randomly select among them
After initial exploration, exploit branch works as before
Seeded randomness preserved for reproducibility

title: Fix ServingCanvas visual lie — dots animate continuously but simulation is discrete
description:
MLDesignModule.tsx:1926: `const phase = ((animTick * 0.12 + d * 0.2) % 1)` creates continuously flowing dots regardless of simulation step. The dots suggest constant traffic flow, but the simulation produces discrete batched steps. This is a visual lie — students may think traffic is flowing when the simulation is between steps.

Fix: Tie dot animation to the current step's request count. Between steps, dots should pause. During a step transition, dots should flow from router to model boxes and then stop.
acceptanceCriteria: Dots only animate during step transitions, not continuously
Between simulation steps, the visualization is static
Number of dots proportional to the current step's traffic count
Animation pauses when simulation is paused or complete

title: Add missing legends to all 7 visualizers that lack them
description:
V4 Legend scored 0 for 7 of 9 visualizers. No legend for: ScatterPlot (blue=class 0, orange=class 1), NetworkDiagram (blue edge=positive weight, orange=negative, thickness=magnitude), DecisionBoundary (color scale from class 0 to class 1), CNN Demo (blue=active window, yellow=current output cell), FeatureStoreCanvas (node colors by type, glow=active), DropoutDemo (blue=active, dashed=dropped). Only PipelineCanvas has a partial legend.

Add compact legends to each visualizer, either as a small overlay in the bottom-left corner or as a sidebar section.
acceptanceCriteria: ScatterPlot has legend: blue circle = class 0, orange circle = class 1
NetworkDiagram has legend: blue line = positive weight, orange = negative, thickness = magnitude
DecisionBoundary has gradient color bar from blue to orange
CNN Demo has legend: blue highlight = current window, yellow = output cell
FeatureStoreCanvas has legend for node types and glow meaning
All legends use consistent styling and don't obscure the visualization

title: Add neuron activation coloring to NetworkDiagram
description:
NetworkDiagram at MLDesignModule.tsx:1104-1119 shows all neurons as the same fill color (#1e293b) regardless of their activation values. The activation data IS available (returned in `lastActivations` from onEpoch) but never used to color neurons.

Fix: After training, color each neuron's fill based on its activation value (0=dark, 1=bright). This would let students SEE which neurons 'fire' for different inputs — a key insight for understanding how neural networks decompose features.

Reference: 3Blue1Brown's NN visualization uses exactly this technique (neurons glow brighter with higher activation).
acceptanceCriteria: Neuron fill color maps to activation value: 0=dark (base), 1=bright blue/orange
Input layer neurons colored by input feature values
Hidden layer neurons colored by their activation after forward pass
Output neuron colored by prediction value
Colors update after training completes
Brightness mapping makes it immediately obvious which neurons are 'active'

title: Increase DecisionBoundary heatmap opacity for better visibility
description:
DecisionBoundary at MLDesignModule.tsx:1179 uses rgba with 0.35 alpha for the heatmap. This makes the decision boundary very faint — students may not notice the boundary forming during training. The data points (drawn on top at full opacity) dominate the visual.

Fix: Increase heatmap alpha to 0.6-0.7. Or use a two-pass approach: heatmap at 0.5 alpha, then data points with white stroke for contrast.
acceptanceCriteria: Decision boundary heatmap is clearly visible against background
Data points still readable on top of the heatmap
Boundary between classes is visually obvious
Color transition from blue to orange is smooth and educational

title: Add pseudocode display for neural network forward pass and backpropagation
description:
ZERO concepts in the module have pseudocode. For a learning platform, pseudocode is how students translate visual understanding into implementable knowledge. The NN forward pass and backprop are the most important algorithms to show.

Add pseudocode panel (bottom panel tab or toggle) showing:

FORWARD PASS:
```
for each layer l:
  for each neuron j in layer l:
    z_j = bias_j + Σ(input_i × weight_ij)
    activation_j = f(z_j)  // f = ReLU, sigmoid, etc.
  output = activations of last layer
```

BACKPROPAGATION:
```
for each layer l (in reverse):
  delta_j = error × f'(activation_j)
  for each weight:
    weight -= learning_rate × delta_j × input_i
```

Highlight the current line during training step-through (when/if step-through mode is added).
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode panel accessible from bottom panel tab or sidebar toggle
Forward pass pseudocode matches neural-net.ts:98-117 logic
Backprop pseudocode matches neural-net.ts:163-208 logic
Pseudocode uses standard mathematical notation (Σ, f(), f'())
Pseudocode is syntax-highlighted and readable at small sizes

title: Add pseudocode display for A/B test z-test and bandit strategies
description:
Add pseudocode for the statistical and bandit algorithms:

Z-TEST:
```
p_pool = (rate_A × n + rate_B × n) / (2n)
SE = sqrt(p_pool × (1 - p_pool) × (2/n))
z = (rate_B - rate_A) / SE
p_value = 2 × (1 - Φ(|z|))  // Φ = normal CDF
significant = p_value < 0.05
```

EPSILON-GREEDY:
```
if random() < ε:
  arm = random_arm()  // explore
else:
  arm = argmax(estimated_values)  // exploit
reward = pull(arm)
update estimate: est[arm] = total_reward / pulls
```

UCB1:
```
for each arm:
  UCB = avg_reward + sqrt(2 × ln(t) / pulls)
arm = argmax(UCB)  // optimism under uncertainty
```
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode panel for A/B testing mode shows z-test formula step by step
Pseudocode for each bandit strategy visible in Experimentation mode
Formulas use standard notation (Σ, argmax, sqrt)
Variables match the implementation names where possible
Each line has a brief inline comment explaining the purpose

title: Rename neural-net.ts variables for educational clarity
description:
neural-net.ts uses short variable names that prioritize brevity over teaching:
- `s` for sample index (line 141) → rename to `sampleIdx`
- `l` for layer index (line 181, 198) → rename to `layerIdx`
- `j` for neuron index → rename to `neuronIdx` (or keep j but add comment)
- `outIdx` is good (descriptive)
- `layerAct` is good

Also add inline 'why' comments:
- Line 175: `// Clip gradient to [-5, 5] to prevent exploding gradients`
- Line 203: `// weight update: w -= lr * delta * input (gradient descent step)`
- Line 109: `// Guard against NaN from weight corruption — output 0 as safe fallback`

This makes the code serve as a readable reference implementation for students.
acceptanceCriteria: Loop variables have descriptive names or explanatory comments
Every non-obvious line has an inline comment explaining WHY
Comments reference the algorithm concept (gradient descent, chain rule)
Code still passes all existing tests
Variable names don't conflict with existing API (layers, weights, etc.)

title: Add Karpathy-style micrograd variant alongside existing NN implementation
description:
Karpathy's micrograd (94 lines) is the gold standard for teaching neural networks from scratch. Our neural-net.ts (230 lines) is a matrix-based implementation — correct but harder to follow the chain rule through.

Add a scalar-valued autograd implementation inspired by micrograd:
- Value class with .data, .grad, .backward()
- Operations (+, *, tanh, exp) that build a computation graph
- .backward() traces the graph to compute gradients

This teaches backpropagation as graph traversal (how it actually works) rather than as matrix multiplications (an optimization). Show both implementations side-by-side.

Source: github.com/karpathy/micrograd
acceptanceCriteria: Autograd Value class with data, grad, and backward() method
Basic operations (+, *, relu, sigmoid) build computation graph
backward() computes gradients via topological sort of graph
Can train a simple classifier on Circle/XOR datasets
Side-by-side toggle: 'Matrix implementation' vs 'Scalar autograd' in NN mode

title: Add complexity counters to NN training (operations, multiply-adds, memory)
description:
Zero concepts show complexity metrics (scored 0/3 on T5 across every concept). For NN training, add live counters:
- Forward pass: multiply-add operations per sample (neurons × inputs per layer)
- Backward pass: gradient computations (same count)
- Total FLOPs per epoch: 2 × forward_ops × num_samples
- Memory: parameter count × 4 bytes (float32)
- Wall-clock time per epoch

Show these in the properties panel alongside loss/accuracy. This helps students develop intuition for WHY larger networks train slower.
acceptanceCriteria: Properties panel shows: operations per forward pass, total FLOPs per epoch, parameter memory
Counters update when network architecture changes
Tooltip explains what FLOPs means and why it matters
Comparison text: 'Our network: 17 params. GPT-4: ~1.8 trillion params'
Wall-clock time per epoch shown for tangible reference

title: Add cumulative regret visualization to bandit simulation
description:
The bandit visualization shows estimated arm values but not cumulative regret — the key metric for comparing bandit strategies. Regret = total reward from always playing the best arm minus total reward from the strategy.

Add: line chart below the bar chart showing cumulative regret over rounds. When comparing strategies (future MLD-033), overlay regret curves for visual comparison. This is how the CSE442 interactive tutorial (cse442-17f.github.io/LinUCB/) presents bandit results — regret comparison is the standard pedagogical tool.

Source: CSE442 Interactive Bandit Tutorial
acceptanceCriteria: Cumulative regret line chart shown below or alongside arm value bars
Regret = (best_arm_true_prob × rounds) - total_reward_received
Chart updates in real-time during animation
X-axis = round number, Y-axis = cumulative regret
Tooltip explains what regret means and why lower is better

title: Add displayable Python code for key algorithms
description:
Zero concepts have displayable real code. Add a 'Code' tab in the bottom panel showing the algorithm in Python (the lingua franca of ML). Code should be clean, educational, and runnable:

NN Forward Pass (Python):
```python
def forward(self, x):
    for W, b, activation in self.layers:
        x = activation(x @ W + b)  # matrix multiply + bias + activation
    return x
```

Backpropagation (Python):
```python
def backward(self, y_true, y_pred):
    delta = y_pred - y_true  # output error
    for layer in reversed(self.layers):
        delta = delta * activation_derivative(layer.output)
        layer.weight_grad = layer.input.T @ delta
        delta = delta @ layer.weights.T  # propagate error backward
```

Show Python alongside the TypeScript implementation so students can see both.
acceptanceCriteria: Bottom panel has a 'Code' tab showing Python implementation
Python code is clean, runnable, and matches the TypeScript logic
At least 3 algorithms have Python code: forward pass, backprop, z-test
Code is syntax-highlighted
Copy-to-clipboard button on the code block

title: Add step-by-step forward pass mode with per-neuron computation display
description:
Currently training runs as a batch — user clicks Train and sees the end result. There's no way to see a SINGLE forward pass through the network on a SINGLE data point with computations shown.

Add a 'Step Through' mode:
1. User selects a data point (click on scatter plot)
2. Forward pass animates: input values → weighted sum → activation → next layer
3. Each neuron shows its computation: z = 0.3×0.5 + (-0.2)×0.8 + 0.1 = 0.05, a = sigmoid(0.05) = 0.512
4. Final output shown with classification result
5. Optional: backward pass shows gradient flow back through the same path

This is the single most requested feature for understanding neural networks (3Blue1Brown approach, Karpathy approach). Source: 3Blue1Brown 'What is a Neural Network?' — the neuron-by-neuron forward pass animation is what made it click for millions.
acceptanceCriteria: User can click a data point to trigger step-through mode
Forward pass animates layer by layer, neuron by neuron
Each neuron shows its weighted sum and activation computation
Output neuron shows classification result with confidence
User can step forward/backward through the computation
Optional backward pass shows gradient values on each connection
